note: Two queries per invoice inside a loop over an org-month the comment pins at ~4,000 invoices: ~8,000 round trips per request. Canonical N+1, must-block.
SELECT * on a table whose two body columns average ~250 KB per row, for a results view that renders id/title/snippet: ~12 MB shipped per 50-row search. Must project columns.
The diff removes keyset pagination (take/cursor/skip) and returns the full org history of a table the adjacent comment pins at millions of rows — guaranteed OOM/latency cliff.

Mined shape (per-invocation acquisition of a resource that should be a reused process-wide one): a fresh pg Client + TCP/auth handshake per request on an endpoint polled every 2 s by ~500 sessions (~250 connects/s). Borderline because the client is tidily closed in finally.

Mined shape (cache keyed on a per-invocation value so entries never converge): keying on req.originalUrl while the client appends a `_=<epoch-ms>` cache-buster (stated in the adjacent comment) drops the hit rate to ~0, so the 700 ms 1.2M-row scan runs on every 5 s poll. The right key is orgId:window.

reasonPattern: sequential|serial|one (at a time|by one)|await(s|ed)? (in|inside|per)|concurren|parallel|promise\.all|queue|offload|background|worker|10 min|blocks? the request
note: Serial awaited fetch per subscriber inside the request handler: 2,000 deliveries x ~300 ms = ~10 minutes holding the request open. Independent deliveries must be offloaded/bounded-concurrent. Adversarial: await-in-for-of reads as idiomatic, nothing pattern-matches as a DB smell.
Textbook list endpoint: clamped take, keyset cursor, explicit select, stable tiebreak ordering. Triggers db-query/pagination/indexing/async/response items by regex but there is nothing to block.

The canonical N+1 FIX: one findMany with an id list plus an in-memory Map join, on a table capped at 50 rows. The .map + await sequence trips the n-plus-one regex, but there is exactly one query per table.

Correct cache-aside: bounded 5-min TTL, explicit delete-on-write invalidation, per-org key. Borderline because caching diffs invite TTL/staleness quibbles, but the strategy is exactly what the brief asks for.

Query-inside-a-for-loop pattern-matches N+1, but the loop is over a hardcoded 3-element const array and runs once at process start — three indexed single-row lookups at boot, not a request-path cliff.

The diff moves Pool construction from module scope into a function called inside handlers — pattern-matches per-request pool creation — but the `if (pool === null)` memoization (synchronous, no await between check and assign) makes it a process-wide singleton. Test-friendliness refactor, nothing to block.

Minimal pair of beperf-nplusone-statement-loop: same base and endpoint, but the per-invoice loop is replaced by one findMany-with-id-list plus one groupBy — three queries total regardless of invoice count.
readFileSync inside a request handler blocks the event loop for every concurrent request; the file shows the boot-time async pattern the handler should reuse. Gold for the new sync-io item.
